articles.title: Remembering a Music Star: Dot Rotten
articles.summary: Dot Rotten was a famous grime music star who died at 37 years old. His family told the BBC about his death, and he might have died in Gambia. He was born Joseph Ellis in London and started making music when he was just seven years old. He made a big hit song called Overload in 2012, which he said came from his feelings of sadness. Dot Rotten worked with many other singers like Ed Sheeran and Cher Lloyd. He changed his name from Young Dot to Dot Rotten, which had a special meaning. People are sharing kind words to remember him for his music and how he helped shape grime music.

articles.title: Why Are Gas Prices Going Up?
articles.summary: Gas prices are going up in the UK because of a war in the Middle East. This war started in late February and has made it harder to get oil, which is used to make petrol and diesel for cars. When oil is harder to get, it costs more, and that makes filling up your car more expensive. Since the war began, oil prices have jumped a lot, from $73 to $106 per barrel. Experts say that for every $10 increase in oil, gas prices go up by about 7p per litre. Right now, petrol costs around 137.51p per litre and diesel is about 150.97p. People think prices will keep rising, with petrol maybe hitting 140p soon and diesel going to 160p. The UK buys most of its oil from other countries like the US and Norway, so when global prices go up, we pay more too.

articles.title: Asian Countries Limit Fuel Prices as Oil Gets Expensive
articles.summary: Some countries in Asia are putting a limit on how much fuel can cost because oil prices are going up. This is happening because there is a war in the Middle East that makes it hard to get oil. Oil is a type of energy that cars and machines use. When oil is hard to get, it becomes more expensive. Countries like South Korea and Thailand are saying they will not let fuel prices go too high. They want to help people afford fuel for their cars and homes. Other countries like Vietnam and the Philippines are also doing things to save energy. For example, the Philippines has a shorter work week to use less energy. This is important because high fuel prices can make things cost more for everyone.

articles.title: Emma Raducanu Loses a Quick Tennis Match
articles.summary: Emma Raducanu, a tennis player from Britain, lost a match to Amanda Anisimova in just 52 minutes at Indian Wells in California. Anisimova won easily with scores of 6-1 and 6-1. Raducanu said she needs to be more aggressive when playing top players because they hit the ball harder and faster. She felt behind during the game and wants to use her strengths better. Raducanu is working with a new coach to improve her game and get back to her best form, like when she won the US Open in 2021. She is staying positive and focusing on her practice. Anisimova will play another match next.

articles.title: Rihanna's House Shot at in Beverly Hills
articles.summary: In Beverly Hills, a famous singer named Rihanna had her home hit by gunfire. Police say a woman in a car fired seven shots at the house and then drove away quickly. No one was hurt, and Rihanna was inside at the time. Officers found bullet casings and caught the suspect about eight miles away. Rihanna is a pop star from Barbados who has three children and runs businesses like Fenty Beauty. This scary event happened on a Sunday afternoon, and police are still investigating why it occurred.

articles.title: Magnets That Act Like Super Material Graphene
articles.summary: Engineers at a university have discovered something cool about magnets and a special material called graphene. Graphene is a thin, strong material that scientists study a lot because it can help make better electronics. The engineers found that magnets in thin films can behave like the electrons in graphene, which move in waves. They made a magnetic system with tiny holes in a honeycomb shape and saw that the magnetic waves act similarly to graphene's electron waves. This connection could help make smaller devices for things like radios and phones. The lead scientist, Bobby Kaman, was surprised by how well this worked. This discovery might lead to new ways to design technology in the future.
